ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bún Thịt Nướng. FOODON_03311737 (rice noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice noodles, cooked) is used as the closest parent. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the composite Bún Thịt Nướng dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising a bowl of dry rice vermicelli topped with grilled pork slices, one or two crispy spring rolls, fresh herbs, pickled vegetables, roasted peanuts, scallion oil, and sweet-sour fish sauce dressing on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Bún Thịt Nướng — component-based nutrient estimation using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for rice vermicelli (cooked), grilled pork, fried spring roll, fresh herbs, peanuts, and fish sauce; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for Vietnamese dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish Bún Thịt Nướng as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis: approximately 45% cooked rice vermicelli, 20% grilled pork, 10% fried spring roll, 10% fresh vegetables and herbs, 5% roasted peanuts, 5% scallion oil, and 5% fish sauce dressing. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used: cooked rice noodles (FDC ID 168936), grilled pork loin (FDC ID 167903), fried spring roll (FDC ID 169238 as closest analogue), roasted peanuts (FDC ID 172430), fish sauce (FDC ID 175148). Values represent a per-100g composite of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli (cooked) GI ~53; composite dish GI estimated from dominant carbohydrate source
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite Bún Thịt Nướng dish. The GI of 53 is derived from the dominant carbohydrate source, cooked rice vermicelli, which has a published GI of approximately 53 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The protein and fat components from grilled pork and spring roll will further attenuate the postprandial glycemic response. Glycemic load per 400 g serving calculated as: (53 × 70 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.1, rounded to 37.0.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce dressing and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and limit the dressing portion, as the dish can contribute approximately 1,680 mg sodium per 400 g serving, which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli as the dominant carbohydrate source
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 400 g serving carries a glycemic load of approximately 37, driven by the rice vermicelli base; portion control and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables can help moderate postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from grilled pork and fried spring roll, combined with high sodium from fish sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size due to the combined contribution of saturated fat (approximately 6.4 g per serving) from the pork and fried spring roll, and high sodium from the fish sauce dressing, both of which are established cardiovascular risk factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Thịt Nướng (Dry Vermicelli with Grilled Pork and Crispy Spring Roll) is a classic Vietnamese noodle dish widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Singapore. The dish consists of thin, dry rice vermicelli noodles served at room temperature in a bowl, topped with slices of char-grilled marinated pork (thịt nướng), one or two crispy deep-fried spring rolls (chả giò), fresh herbs such as mint and perilla, shredded lettuce, bean sprouts, pickled daikon and carrot, crushed roasted peanuts, and fragrant scallion oil. The defining element of the dish is the sweet-sour-savoury fish sauce dressing (nước chấm), made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli, which is poured over or served alongside the bowl. The grilled pork is typically marinated in lemongrass, garlic, fish sauce, and sugar before being grilled over charcoal or a gas grill, imparting a smoky, caramelised flavour. The spring rolls add a contrasting crispy texture. Nutritionally, the dish provides a moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with a balanced macronutrient profile of 7.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat, and 17.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. The rice vermicelli base has a low glycemic index of approximately 53, making the dish moderately suitable for glycemic management when portion-controlled. However, the sodium content is high due to the fish sauce dressing, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and rich in fresh herbs, contributing small amounts of vitamins A and C. It is a culturally significant Vietnamese dish that has become a staple of Vietnamese cuisine in Singapore's food landscape.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish Bún Thịt Nướng as served in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (cooked rice vermicelli) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and cross-referenced, but direct measurement of the composite dish from an approved primary source (e.g., HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA) was not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-based estimates for the composite dish as typically served in Singapore Vietnamese restaurants. The dish composition may vary by restaurant. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for the individual components of this dish and is not expected to be a significant contributor. 3. Sodium content is high due to the fish sauce dressing; the estimate of 420 mg per 100 g reflects a moderate dressing application. Actual sodium may be higher if full dressing is consumed. 4. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. 5. GI of 53 is based on the cooked rice vermicelli component per the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite dish GI may be slightly lower due to protein and fat attenuation. 6. Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (53 × 70 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 37.1. 7. The food is classified under Singapore cuisine in the input record but the dish is of Vietnamese origin; region_of_origin reflects this. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from component analysis and carry moderate uncertainty. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source database entries used for this estimation.
